title: Hello, Goodbye, and Thank You Around the World
description: A globe-trotting, slightly silly tour of the three phrases every traveller actually needs — *hello*, *goodbye*, and *thank you* — across 40-plus languages. Each question pairs the right word with its original spelling, an English-friendly pronunciation, and the surprising literal meaning hiding inside it: why `ciao` means 'slave', why Japanese tiptoes around `sayōnara`, and why your breezy English 'bye' is secretly a blessing. Explanation-first, so you learn the nuance even when you guess wrong.
author: Quizbun

questions.title: How do you say **hello** in Spanish?
questions.description: Careful — three of these are 'hello' in *other* languages.
questions.explanation: **Hola** (*OH-lah* — the `h` is silent, as it always is in Spanish) is your all-purpose Spanish hello. The impostors are Italian (`Ciao`), Portuguese (`Olá`), and French (`Bonjour`). Spanish almost never aspirates `h`, so saying *HOH-lah* instantly marks you as a beginner.

questions.title: What does the Spanish goodbye **adiós** literally mean?
questions.explanation: **Adiós** (*ah-DYOHS*) is `a Dios`, '[I commend you] to God.' Because it once meant a weighty, possibly final parting, many speakers prefer `hasta luego` ('see you later') for everyday byes; `adiós` can sound like you don't expect to meet again. Fun fact: English *goodbye* hides the same idea — it's a worn-down 'God be with ye.'

questions.title: Which word is **thank you** in Spanish?
questions.description: The wrong answers are real 'thank you's — in Italian, Portuguese, and French.
questions.explanation: **Gracias** (*GRAH-syahs*) comes from Latin `gratia` ('grace, favour') — the same root behind Italian `grazie` and English *gratitude*. It is always plural in form. Note the clean `s`: most of Latin America says a crisp *s*, while much of Spain pronounces the `c` like the *th* in 'thin' (*GRAH-thyahs*).

questions.title: The standard daytime French greeting **bonjour** literally means…
questions.explanation: **Bonjour** (*bohn-ZHOOR*) is `bon` (good) + `jour` (day), usable all daylight long; after dark you switch to `bonsoir` ('good evening'). The casual `salut` (*sah-LU*) works for both 'hi' AND 'bye', and comes from Latin `salus`, 'health' — literally wishing someone wellness.

questions.title: What does the French goodbye **au revoir** literally mean?
questions.explanation: **Au revoir** (*oh ruh-VWAR*) breaks down to `au` (until the) + `revoir` (seeing again). It belongs to a huge cross-language family of 'see you again' goodbyes: German `auf Wiedersehen`, Italian `arrivederci`, Russian `do svidaniya`. For a casual bye, `salut` or `à plus` do the job.

questions.title: **Merci**, the French 'thank you', is a cousin of which English word?
questions.explanation: **Merci** (*mehr-SEE*) descends from Latin `merces` ('wages, reward'), which drifted toward 'favour, grace, mercy' — so French thanks is kin to English *mercy*. Add `beaucoup` for `merci beaucoup` ('thanks a lot'). It is so handy that Persian and Romanian speakers borrowed `merci` for their own everyday thanks.

questions.title: Which is a normal German **hello**?
questions.description: Two of the wrong answers are German words you may recognise — but neither means 'hello'.
questions.explanation: **Guten Tag** (*GOO-ten TAHK*) is 'good day', the polite all-rounder; casually people say `Hallo` or, in the south, `Servus`. The traps are German words you may know: `Gesundheit` ('health!') follows a sneeze, `Achtung` means 'attention/careful', and `Tschüss` is actually 'bye'.

questions.title: **Auf Wiedersehen** literally promises…
questions.explanation: **Auf Wiedersehen** (*owf VEE-der-zayn*) = `auf` (until) + `wieder` (again) + `sehen` (see), another 'see you again'. On the phone Germans say `auf Wiederhören` ('until we *hear* each other again'). For everyday partings, the breezy `Tschüss` (*chooss*) is far more common.

questions.title: What is **thank you** in German?
questions.explanation: **Danke** (*DAHN-kuh*) is thanks; dress it up as `danke schön` ('thank you kindly') or `danke sehr`. The decoys are everyday words too: `bitte` is 'please' (and also 'you're welcome'!), `Entschuldigung` is 'excuse me/sorry', and `Prost` is 'cheers'.

questions.title: The Italian **ciao** (used for both 'hi' and 'bye') comes from a phrase meaning…
questions.explanation: **Ciao** (*chow*) began as Venetian `s-ciào vostro`, 'I am your slave/servant' — an over-the-top 'at your service' that softened into a casual 'hi/bye'. The same servant-flattery gave Hungarian and Austrian German their `szia` / `servus`. So every breezy `ciao` is a tiny medieval bow.

questions.title: Besides `ciao`, which is a polite Italian **hello**?
questions.explanation: **Buongiorno** (*bwohn-JOR-noh*), 'good day', is the polite greeting; `buonasera` covers the evening. The traps: `arrivederci` is goodbye, `grazie` is thanks, and `prego` is the Swiss-army word for 'you're welcome / please / go ahead / after you'.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Italian?
questions.description: Only one spelling is the actual Italian word.
questions.explanation: **Grazie** (*GRAHT-tsyeh* — two syllables, and that final `-e` is *not* silent) is Italian thanks, from Latin `gratiae`. A classic slip is saying *GRAHT-zee* like an English speaker; Italians sound the `e`. Amp it up with `grazie mille` ('a thousand thanks').

questions.title: What does the Mandarin greeting **你好 (nǐ hǎo)** literally mean?
questions.explanation: **你好** (*nee-how*) is literally `你` (you) + `好` (good). The two third-tone syllables hide a trap: a third tone before another third tone shifts to a rising tone, so it actually comes out *ní hǎo*. For 'hello?' on the phone, Chinese say `喂 (wéi)` instead.

questions.title: The Mandarin goodbye **再见 (zàijiàn)** literally means…
questions.explanation: **再见** (*dzye-jyen*) is `再` (again) + `见` (see) — yet another 'see you again', the same logic as `au revoir`. Younger speakers often just borrow English and say `bái-bái` (拜拜). Tones matter: both syllables are falling fourth tones, a crisp downward *ZYE-JYEN*.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Mandarin Chinese?
questions.description: The decoys are hello, goodbye, and 'sorry'.
questions.explanation: **谢谢** (*syeh-syeh*) is thanks. The polite reply is `不客气 (bú kèqi)`, literally 'don't be so polite/formal'. The decoys are hello, goodbye, and `对不起` ('sorry') — three of the first phrases every learner meets.

questions.title: **こんにちは (konnichiwa)** is a daytime hello — but it's literally an *unfinished sentence*. The leftover beginning means…
questions.explanation: **こんにちは** (*kohn-nee-chee-wah*) is `今日は` ('as for today…'), the opening of an old full greeting like 'as for today, how are you?' that got chopped off, leaving a polite dangling phrase. Spelling nuance: that last syllable is written は (the topic particle *ha*) but pronounced *wa*. Evening swaps it for `こんばんは (konbanwa)`.

questions.title: Why do Japanese speakers often *avoid* saying **さようなら (sayōnara)** to friends and family?

options.text: It can sound final, like a long or permanent farewell

questions.explanation: **さようなら** (*sah-yoh-nah-rah*) grew out of `さようならば` ('well then, if it must be so') and carries a wistful, weighty 'this may be goodbye for a long time' feel. For everyday partings people prefer `じゃあね (jaa ne)` ('well, see ya') or `また (mata)` ('again'). Saying `sayōnara` to your spouse could sound like you are leaving for good.

questions.title: The Japanese **ありがとう (arigatō)** grew from a word that literally meant…

options.text: Difficult to exist (i.e. rare and precious)

questions.explanation: **ありがとう** (*ah-ree-gah-toh*) traces to `有り難い (arigatai)`, 'difficult to be/exist' — a kindness so rare it is precious. Make it polite as `arigatō gozaimasu`. That lengthened final *o* matters; clipping it to a flat 'arigato' sounds brusque.

questions.title: The casual Korean **안녕 (annyeong)** can mean both 'hi' and 'bye'. Its root meaning is…
questions.explanation: **안녕** (*ahn-nyong*) is the Sino-Korean `安寧`, 'peace, tranquillity' — so the greeting literally wishes you peace, like Hebrew `shalom`. Polite up to `안녕하세요 (annyeonghaseyo)`, roughly 'are you at peace?'. Casual `annyeong` works both arriving and leaving, just like `aloha` or `ciao`.

questions.title: Formal Korean has TWO goodbyes. You say **안녕히 가세요 (annyeonghi gaseyo)** — 'go in peace' — to whom?
questions.explanation: Korean splits the farewell by who moves. To the one leaving you say `안녕히 가세요` ('go in peace'); the one leaving says `안녕히 계세요 (annyeonghi gyeseyo)` ('stay in peace') to those who remain. Mix them up and you have told a guest to leave their own house. Turkish, Zulu, Māori and Malay share this stay-vs-go split.

questions.title: Which is a standard polite **thank you** in Korean?
questions.description: The decoys are 'goodbye', 'sorry', and 'I love you'.
questions.explanation: **감사합니다** (*gam-sa-ham-ni-da*) is the formal thanks (Sino-Korean `感謝`, 'feel gratitude'); native-Korean `고맙습니다 (gomapseumnida)` is equally polite. That `ㅂ니다` ending sounds like *-mnida*, not *-bnida*. The decoys: `미안합니다` is 'sorry', `사랑해요` is 'I love you'.

questions.title: The full Russian hello **Здравствуйте (zdravstvuyte)** literally wishes you…
questions.explanation: **Здравствуйте** (*ZDRAST-vooy-tye*) comes from `здоровье` ('health') — basically 'be healthy!'. That brutal `здр-` cluster is a rite of passage; natives often blur it to *ZDRAS-tye*. Among friends you just say `Привет (privet)`, 'hi'.

questions.title: What does the Russian **До свидания (do svidaniya)** literally mean?
questions.explanation: **До свидания** (*da svee-DAH-nya*) is `до` (until) + `свидание` (a meeting) — 'until we meet again', joining the global 'see you again' club. Casually you can say `пока (poka)`, a cheery 'bye for now'. Note `свидание` on its own often means a romantic *date*.

questions.title: The Russian **Спасибо (spasibo)** is a squashed-together version of which phrase?
questions.explanation: **Спасибо** (*spah-SEE-bah*) fused `спаси Бог` ('God save [you]') into one everyday word, so a tiny blessing hides inside every Russian thank-you. For 'thank you very much', add `большое`: `спасибо большое`. The ultra-formal `благодарю (blagodaryu)`, 'I give blessing', still survives in polite speech.

questions.title: The greeting **السلام عليكم (as-salāmu ʿalaykum)** means…
questions.explanation: **السلام عليكم** (*as-sa-LAA-mu a-LAY-kum*), 'peace be upon you', is answered `وعليكم السلام (wa ʿalaykum as-salām)`, 'and upon you, peace'. The casual everyday hello is `مرحبا (marhaba)`, *MAR-ha-ba*. The shared root `s-l-m` ('peace, wholeness') links Arabic to Hebrew `shalom`.

questions.title: The Arabic farewell **مع السلامة (maʿa as-salāma)** literally means…
questions.explanation: **مع السلامة** (*ma-as-sa-LAA-ma*) is `مع` (with) + `السلامة` (safety/peace) — 'go with safety'. There is that `s-l-m` peace-root again, the same one inside `salām` and `Islam`. The reply is often `Allah yusallimak`, 'may God keep you safe'.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Arabic?
questions.description: The decoys are 'you're welcome', 'hello', and 'God willing'.
questions.explanation: **شكرا** (*SHOOK-ran*) is thanks; the reply is `عفوا (ʿafwan)`, 'you're welcome / no pardon needed'. For heartfelt thanks add `جزيلا (jazīlan)`: `shukran jazīlan`, 'thanks abundantly'. The decoy `inshallah` ('God willing') is one of Arabic's most-borrowed phrases worldwide.

questions.title: The Hindi greeting **नमस्ते (namaste)** literally means…
questions.explanation: **नमस्ते** (*nuh-muh-STAY*) is Sanskrit `namaḥ` (bow, salutation) + `te` (to you), usually with palms pressed together (the `añjali` gesture). It works morning, noon and night, for hello and goodbye; the more respectful form is `namaskār`. No handshake required — it aged surprisingly well as a no-contact greeting.

questions.title: Which is a common Hindi/Urdu way to say **goodbye**?
questions.description: The decoys are 'thank you', 'welcome', and 'forgive me'.
questions.explanation: **अलविदा** (*al-vee-DAA*) — a poetic, slightly dramatic goodbye borrowed from Arabic/Persian — is beloved by Bollywood. In daily life `namaste` doubles as a parting, and `phir milenge` ('we'll meet again') is the warm everyday choice. The decoy `svāgat` actually means 'welcome'.

questions.title: What's a frequent surprise about saying **thank you** (धन्यवाद, dhanyavād) in everyday Hindi?

options.text: Among close friends and family it can sound oddly formal or distant
It can only be written, never spoken

questions.explanation: **धन्यवाद** (*DHUN-yuh-vaad*), 'thanks be [to you]', is correct and polite — but between intimates many Indians skip explicit thanks, since a favour for family is simply expected; gushing `dhanyavād` at your mum can sound stiff. In casual speech people often just say the English 'thank you' or the Urdu-flavoured `shukriyā`.

questions.title: Which is **hello** in Portuguese?
questions.explanation: **Olá** (*oh-LAH*) is the standard Portuguese hello; casual Brazilians love `oi`. Don't confuse it with Spanish `hola` (no accent, silent h): Portuguese stresses and accents the final *á*. The European-vs-Brazilian accent gap is huge, but `olá` is safe everywhere Portuguese is spoken.

questions.title: The casual Brazilian goodbye **tchau** is borrowed from which language?
questions.explanation: **Tchau** (*chow*) is just Italian `ciao` re-spelled to fit Portuguese — a legacy of massive Italian immigration to Brazil. The more formal goodbye is `adeus` (*ah-DEOOSH*), literally 'to God', the Portuguese twin of Spanish `adiós`. For 'see you later', say `até logo`.

questions.title: In Portuguese a **man** says `obrigado` and a **woman** says `obrigada` for 'thank you'. Why?

options.text: It's an adjective agreeing with the speaker's own gender
Men and women use different dialects
It agrees with the person being thanked
It's just an old spelling tradition

questions.explanation: **Obrigado / obrigada** (*oh-bree-GAH-doo / -dah*) literally means '[I am] obliged', and as an adjective it agrees with whoever is *speaking* — not the listener. So a woman always says `obrigada`, even when thanking a man. It is the classic Portuguese gotcha that trips up every learner at least once.

questions.title: The Hebrew **שלום (shalom)** serves as hello AND goodbye. What does it literally mean?
questions.explanation: **שלום** (*shah-LOHM*) means 'peace, completeness, wholeness', and works for both greeting and parting, like `aloha`. It shares the Semitic root `š-l-m` with Arabic `salām`. Casual modern Israelis also toss around `היי (hi)` and the breezy Italian-borrowed `צ'או (ciao)`.

questions.title: Besides `shalom`, a very common Hebrew goodbye is **להתראות (lehitra'ot)**, meaning…
questions.explanation: **להתראות** (*le-hit-ra-OT*), 'to seeing one another [again]', is yet another 'see you again' goodbye. Israelis borrow heavily too: you'll hear French-origin `ביי (bye)` and Italian `צ'או (ciao)` constantly. For 'see you soon', add `bekarov`.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Hebrew?
questions.description: The decoys mean 'you're welcome/please', 'sorry', and 'peace'.
questions.explanation: **תודה** (*toh-DAH*) is thanks; pile on `רבה (raba)` for `toda raba`, 'thank you very much'. The reply is `בבקשה (bevakasha)` which, like German `bitte`, covers both 'please' and 'you're welcome'.

questions.title: The informal Greek **γειά σου (yia sou)** — used for both hi and bye — literally means…
questions.explanation: **γειά σου** (*YAH soo*) is 'health to you' (`υγεία`, health), so Greeks literally toast your wellness when they say hi. It flips to plural/polite as `γειά σας (yia sas)`. For 'good morning' use `καλημέρα (kaliméra)`. Same word, hello or goodbye — context decides.

questions.title: The Greek goodbye **αντίο (antío)** was borrowed from Italian and ultimately means…
questions.explanation: **αντίο** (*ah-DEE-oh*) came from Italian `addio` (`a Dio`, 'to God') — the same to-God family as `adiós`, `adeus`, `adieu`. For everyday byes Greeks reuse `γειά σου`. So Greek's dramatic goodbye is actually an Italian import.

questions.title: The Greek **ευχαριστώ (efcharistó)** ('thank you') gave English which church word?
questions.explanation: **ευχαριστώ** (*ef-ha-ree-STOH*) literally means 'I am grateful' — and from the noun `eucharistia` ('thanksgiving') English gets *Eucharist*. It is a mouthful; the reply is `παρακαλώ (parakaló)`, which (like German `bitte`) doubles as 'please'.

questions.title: The Turkish hello **merhaba** is a cousin of which other language's greeting?
questions.explanation: **Merhaba** (*MER-ha-ba*) was borrowed from Arabic `marhaba` ('welcome'), a trace of centuries of Ottoman-Arabic contact. Pronounce all three syllables clearly. A breezier hi is `selam`, itself from Arabic `salām` — yet another peace-root greeting.

questions.title: Turkish has two goodbyes. The person **staying** says **güle güle** ('go smilingly'); what does the person **leaving** say?
questions.explanation: Like Korean and Zulu, Turkish splits the farewell. The one heading out says `hoşça kal` ('stay pleasantly/well'); the one staying behind answers `güle güle` ('go laughing/smiling'). Say `güle güle` as *you* leave and you have cheerfully ordered yourself to go.

questions.title: A famously tongue-twisting Turkish 'thank you' is **teşekkür ederim**. What's a simpler everyday alternative?
questions.explanation: **Teşekkür ederim** (*teh-shek-KEWR eh-deh-rim*) is the full, slightly intimidating thanks (the `teşekkür` part is itself an Arabic loan). Friends often just say `sağ ol` ('be alive/healthy') or the plural `teşekkürler`. The decoy `görüşürüz` means 'see you'.

questions.title: The Thai **สวัสดี (sawatdi)** works for hi and bye and was coined in the 1930s from a Sanskrit word meaning…
questions.explanation: **สวัสดี** (*sa-wat-DEE*) was popularised in the 1930s from Sanskrit `svasti`, 'well-being, blessing' (the same auspicious root behind the ancient `svastika` symbol). It is paired with the `wai` — palms together, slight bow — and softened by a politeness particle: men add `ครับ (khráp)`, women add `ค่ะ (khâ)`.

questions.title: What's the most common way to say 'goodbye' in everyday Thai?

options.text: Reuse สวัสดี (sawatdi) — the same word as hello
A completely separate formal word only
There is no goodbye in Thai

questions.explanation: Thai keeps it simple: `สวัสดี (sawatdi)` covers both hello and goodbye, like `aloha` or `ciao`, usually with the `wai` gesture and a `khráp` / `khâ` particle. There is also `ลาก่อน (laa gòn)` for a more formal 'farewell', but day to day, `sawatdi` does double duty.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Thai?
questions.description: The decoys are 'hello', 'no worries', and 'how much?'.

options.text: ไม่เป็นไร (mai pen rai)
เท่าไหร่ (thao rai)

questions.explanation: **ขอบคุณ** (*kawp-KOON*) is thanks, again finished with `khráp` (male) or `khâ` (female) for politeness. Thai is tonal, so the pitch pattern is part of the word, not decoration. The lovely decoy `ไม่เป็นไร (mai pen rai)` — 'no worries / never mind' — is practically a national philosophy.

questions.title: Why is saying 'hello' in Vietnamese trickier than memorising one word?

options.text: The greeting changes with the listener's age and your relationship
Vietnamese has no word for hello

questions.explanation: **Xin chào** (*sin chow*) is the safe textbook hello, but real Vietnamese greetings swap in a pronoun matched to the other person's age and status — `chào anh` (older male), `chào chị` (older female), `chào em` (younger), and so on. Vietnamese is tonal too, so `chào` must carry its falling tone. (Despite sounding like `ciao`, the link is debated.)

questions.title: The Vietnamese goodbye **tạm biệt** literally means…
questions.explanation: **Tạm biệt** (*tahm byet*) is `tạm` (temporary) + `biệt` (separate), 'part for now' — a gentle 'this isn't forever'. It is a bit formal; casually people just reuse `chào` (`chào anh!`) on the way out, again matching the pronoun to the listener.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Vietnamese?
questions.description: The decoys are 'hello', 'goodbye', and 'sorry'.
questions.explanation: **Cảm ơn** (*gam uhn*) is thanks; add `nhiều` for 'thanks a lot', or a pronoun like `cảm ơn anh/chị` to be polite. As always in Vietnamese, the tones are doing real work — flatten them and the meaning wobbles. The decoys are 'hello', 'goodbye', and `xin lỗi` ('sorry').

questions.title: Tourists learn **jambo** for hello, but what do locals more naturally say?
questions.explanation: **Jambo** is the simplified hello aimed at visitors; among themselves Swahili speakers ask `Habari?` ('[any] news?') or `Hujambo?` ('you have no problems?', answered `Sijambo`). The decoys: `asante` is thanks, `kwaheri` is goodbye, and `rafiki` is 'friend' (yes, *that* Rafiki).

questions.title: The Swahili **asante** ('thank you') appears in which famous Disney song lyric?

options.text: Asante sana, squash banana (The Lion King)
Can you feel the love tonight

questions.explanation: **Asante** (*ah-SAHN-teh*) is thanks; `asante sana` is 'thank you very much' — Rafiki's little chant. The film is a Swahili goldmine: `simba` = lion, `rafiki` = friend, `nala` = gift, and `hakuna matata` really does mean 'no worries'.

questions.title: What does the Swahili goodbye **kwaheri** convey?
questions.explanation: **Kwaheri** (*kwah-HEH-ree*) builds on `heri` ('blessing, good fortune', itself from Arabic `khayr`, 'good'), so you literally send someone off 'with goodness'. Swahili is a Bantu language with a thick layer of Arabic loanwords from centuries of Indian Ocean trade, and `kwaheri` is a tidy example.

questions.title: **Aloha** famously means hello, goodbye, *and* love. Its deeper literal sense is…
questions.explanation: **Aloha** (*ah-LOH-hah*) joins `alo` (presence, face, to share) with `hā` (breath) — 'the sharing of breath', echoed in the traditional `honi` greeting where two people touch foreheads and inhale together. It carries love, compassion and mutual respect, so it is far warmer than a throwaway 'hi'.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Hawaiian?
questions.description: The decoys mean 'hello/love', 'family', and 'porch'.
questions.explanation: **Mahalo** (*mah-HAH-loh*) is thanks; `mahalo nui loa` is 'thank you very much'. You'll spot it on Hawaii's trash cans ('Mahalo for not littering'). The decoy `ohana` ('family, including chosen family') went global thanks to a certain blue alien movie.

questions.title: Besides reusing `aloha`, a Hawaiian way to say 'until we meet again' is…
questions.explanation: **A hui hou** (*ah HOO-ee HOH*) means 'until we meet again' — Hawaii's gentle 'see you later', though `aloha` alone also covers goodbye. The decoys: `pau hana` = 'work is done' (happy hour!), and `e komo mai` = 'welcome, come in'.

questions.title: The versatile Māori greeting **kia ora** literally means…
questions.explanation: **Kia ora** (*kee-ah OR-ah*) is literally 'be well / have life', and it is gloriously multipurpose: hello, thanks, cheers, 'I agree', even 'right on'. It is everyday New Zealand English now, used by Māori and non-Māori alike. For a bigger welcome there is `kia ora koutou` (to many).

questions.title: Māori farewells split by movement: the person **leaving** is told **haere rā** ('go'); what does the person **staying** hear?
questions.explanation: Like Korean, Turkish and Zulu, Māori picks the goodbye by who moves: `haere rā` ('go, then') to the one departing, `e noho rā` ('stay, then') to the one remaining. Casually, Kiwis say `ka kite (anō)`, 'see you (again)'. The decoy `haere mai` is actually 'welcome / come here'.

questions.title: What's a common Māori way to say **thank you**?
questions.description: Yes — the hello word doubles as thanks. The decoys are 'goodbye', 'love', and 'family'.
questions.explanation: **Kia ora** moonlights as a casual 'thanks', while `ngā mihi` (*ngah MEE-hee*, 'the acknowledgements') is a warmer, more formal thank-you. The decoys carry their own weight: `aroha` is love/compassion (cousin to Hawaiian `aloha`) and `whānau` is extended family.

questions.title: The Zulu greeting **sawubona** literally means…
questions.explanation: **Sawubona** (*sah-woo-BOH-nah*) literally means 'I see you' (plural `sanibonani` for several people) — and it means truly seeing the other person's humanity, an idea tied to `ubuntu`. The reply is `yebo` ('yes') or `ngikhona` ('I am here'). It is the real-world cousin of that 'I see you' line from Avatar.

questions.title: Zulu farewells depend on who moves. To someone **leaving** you say **hamba kahle** ('go well'); to someone **staying** you say…
questions.explanation: Zulu joins the stay-vs-go goodbye club: `hamba kahle` ('go well') to the one departing, `sala kahle` ('stay well') to the one remaining (`kahle` means 'well/nicely'). Same split logic as Korean, Turkish and Māori — a surprisingly common idea across unrelated languages.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Zulu?
questions.description: The decoys are 'I see you (hello)', 'go well (bye)', and 'yes'.
questions.explanation: **Ngiyabonga** (*ngee-yah-BOHN-gah*) is 'I give thanks' (`ngi-` = I, `bonga` = to thank/praise). That word-initial `ng-` is tough for English speakers — practise a soft 'sing'-style *ng*. In neighbouring Xhosa, thanks is `enkosi`.

questions.title: The casual Polish hello **cześć** is notoriously hard to pronounce. It sounds roughly like…
questions.explanation: **Cześć** (*cheshch*) crams together sounds English keeps apart, so it is a classic 'unpronounceable' Polish word; it works for both hi and bye among friends. The formal greeting is `dzień dobry` ('good day'). Polish spelling looks fierce but is rule-consistent once you learn the digraphs.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Polish?
questions.description: The decoys are 'please/you're welcome', 'sorry', and 'goodbye'.
questions.explanation: **Dziękuję** (*jen-KOO-yeh*) is thanks (cousin to German `danke` and Czech `děkuji`); casual `dzięki` is 'thanks'. The reply `proszę`, like German `bitte`, means both 'please' and 'you're welcome'. The decoy `do widzenia` ('until we see') is goodbye.

questions.title: What's a very common informal Dutch goodbye?
questions.description: One decoy is 'hi', one is 'please/here you go', one means 'tasty/nice'.
questions.explanation: **Doei** (*DOO-ee*) is the cheery everyday 'bye'; `tot ziens` ('until we see') is the more formal version, and `hoi` is the matching casual 'hi'. The decoy `lekker` ('tasty, nice, good') is one of the most Dutch words there is — applied to food, weather, naps and people alike.

questions.title: Why do Dutch learners meet **two** words for 'thank you' — `dank je` and `dank u`?

options.text: One is Dutch, one is German

questions.explanation: Dutch, like many European languages, splits 'you' into casual `je/jij` and polite `u`, so thanks comes as `dank je` (friends) or `dank u` (strangers, elders, customers). `Bedankt` is a handy neutral 'thanks' that sidesteps the choice. Pile on `wel` for `dank je wel`, 'thanks very much'.

questions.title: Which is **hello** in Persian (Farsi)?
questions.description: The decoys are 'thanks', 'goodbye', and 'yes'.
questions.explanation: **سلام** (*sa-LAAM*) — the same Semitic 'peace' root as Arabic `salām` and Hebrew `shalom` — is the everyday Persian hello; a warmer native option is `dorood`. Persian is Indo-European (a cousin of English!), but centuries of contact layered in many Arabic loans like this one.

questions.title: Which French word do Persian speakers very commonly use for 'thank you'?
questions.explanation: Persians cheerfully say `مرسی (merci)` — borrowed straight from French — alongside native options like `ممنون (mamnun)` and the formal `متشکرم (moteshakkeram)`. French was once the prestige language of the Iranian elite, and `merci` simply stuck. The goodbye, by contrast, stays proudly Persian: `khodāhāfez`, 'may God protect you'.

questions.title: The Indonesian/Malay **terima kasih** ('thank you') literally means…
questions.explanation: **Terima kasih** (*teh-REE-ma KAH-see*) is `terima` (receive) + `kasih` (love, affection), 'receive [my] love'. Indonesian is wonderfully logical and toneless, which makes it one of the friendlier languages for English speakers to start. Reply with `sama-sama` ('same to you / you're welcome').

questions.title: Indonesian/Malay has two goodbyes. You tell the person **staying** `selamat tinggal` ('stay well'); what do you say to the one **leaving**?
questions.explanation: Another stay-vs-go split: `selamat tinggal` ('stay safe') to whoever remains, `selamat jalan` ('safe journey') to whoever departs. `Selamat` ('safe, blessed', from Arabic `salāmah`) prefixes nearly everything: `selamat pagi` (good morning), `selamat makan` (enjoy your meal), even `selamat ulang tahun` (happy birthday).

questions.title: The Tagalog hello **kumusta** was borrowed and reshaped from which language?
questions.explanation: **Kumusta** (*koo-moos-TAH*) is Spanish `¿cómo está?` ('how are you?') worn down into a one-word hello — a fossil of 300-plus years of Spanish rule in the Philippines. Filipinos also just say 'Hi' / 'Hello'. Spanish fingerprints are everywhere in Tagalog, including the numbers and the days of the week.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Tagalog/Filipino?
questions.description: The decoys are 'goodbye', 'hello', and 'yes'.
questions.explanation: **Salamat** (*sah-LAH-mat*) is thanks — and, like Malay `selamat`, it traces back through trade and Islam to Arabic `salāmah` ('peace, safety'). Add `po` for respect (`salamat po`) and `marami` for 'many thanks' (`maraming salamat`). The decoy `paalam` is the heartfelt goodbye.

questions.title: The Irish greeting **Dia duit** literally means…
questions.explanation: **Dia duit** (*JEE-ah gwit*) literally means 'God [be] to you', and the traditional reply doubles down: `Dia is Muire duit`, 'God and Mary to you' — to which one might add `agus Pádraig` ('and Patrick'), a gentle game of one-upmanship. Irish spelling looks daunting, but the greetings are pure blessing.

questions.title: The Irish goodbye **slán** comes from a word meaning…
questions.explanation: **Slán** (*slawn*) means 'safe, sound, healthy', so the goodbye is a compact 'stay safe'. Spell it out as `slán abhaile` ('safe home') to someone heading off, or `slán go fóill` ('bye for now'). Short word, warm wish.

questions.title: Which everyday Czech greeting (used for both hi and bye) sounds delightfully like a pirate's cry?
questions.explanation: **Ahoj** (*AH-hoy*) is the casual Czech hi/bye — and yes, it sounds exactly like the English sailor's 'ahoy'. Cheeky folk tales link it to sailors or even a Latin backronym, but its real path was probably through that very nautical shout. The formal greeting is `dobrý den` ('good day').

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Czech?
questions.description: The decoys are 'please/you're welcome', 'excuse me', and 'goodbye'.
questions.explanation: **Děkuji** (*DYEH-koo-yih*) is thanks (casual `díky`), a Slavic cousin of German `danke` and Polish `dziękuję`. `Prosím`, like German `bitte`, means both 'please' and 'you're welcome'. The formal goodbye `na shledanou` is — you guessed it — another 'until we see again'.

questions.title: Which is an informal Ukrainian **hello**?
questions.description: The decoys are 'thank you', 'goodbye', and 'please'.
questions.explanation: **Привіт** (*pree-VEET*) is the casual hello; the polite version is `Доброго дня` ('good day'). Ukrainian and Russian are related but distinct — `pryvit` vs Russian `privet`, `dobroho dnia` vs `dobryy den` — and the differences carry real cultural weight.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Ukrainian?
questions.description: The decoys are 'hello', 'yes', and 'excuse me'.
questions.explanation: **Дякую** (*DYAH-koo-yoo*) is thanks — and notice it patterns with Polish `dziękuję` and Czech `děkuji`, all from a Slavic borrowing of German `danke`, rather than with Russian `spasibo`. The goodbye `до побачення` ('until we see') is yet another 'see you again'.

questions.title: How did a Roman say **hello** to one person?
questions.explanation: **Salvē** (*SAL-way*) literally means 'be well/healthy' (to several people, `salvēte`). The poetic `avē` ('hail!') is the one you know from `Avē, Caesar`. So Latin greetings, like Russian and Greek ones, are little health wishes — ancestors of all those 'salut'-style hellos.

questions.title: A Roman parting word, **valē**, literally means…
questions.explanation: **Valē** (*WAH-lay*, plural `valēte`) means 'be strong, fare well' — the ancestor, in spirit, of English *farewell*. It is why a `valediction` is a goodbye and a `valedictorian` gives the farewell speech. Latin greeted you with health (`salvē`) and dismissed you with strength (`valē`).

questions.title: A casual Finnish hello is **moi**. What's a fun thing Finns do with it to say *goodbye*?
questions.explanation: Finns turn the casual `moi` ('hi') into a goodbye simply by doubling it: `moi moi`! `Hei` works the same way as `hei hei`. It is about the most charming farewell in Europe. The more formal goodbye is `näkemiin`, 'until [we] see'.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Finnish?
questions.description: The decoys are 'hi', 'sorry/excuse me', and 'goodbye'.
questions.explanation: **Kiitos** (*KEE-tohs*) is thanks; `kiitos paljon` is 'thanks a lot'. Finnish is not Indo-European at all — it is Uralic, related to Estonian and Hungarian, not to Swedish or Russian — so its words look refreshingly unfamiliar. The decoys are 'hi', 'sorry', and 'goodbye'.

questions.title: The Swedish hello **hej** becomes a goodbye how?
questions.explanation: **Hej** (*hey*) is the friendly Swedish hello — and `hej då` (*hey doh*, roughly 'hi then') is the matching 'bye'; `hej hej` also works for either. Scandinavian greetings are wonderfully low-effort, and Swedish thanks, `tack`, is just one syllable.

questions.title: The casual Norwegian goodbye **ha det** is short for a phrase meaning…
questions.explanation: **Ha det** (*HA deh*) is short for `ha det bra`, 'have it good' — a cousin of English 'take care'. The hello is `hei` and thanks is `takk`. Norwegian, Swedish and Danish are close enough that speakers can often muddle through each other's languages.

questions.title: Which is a Danish way to say **goodbye**?
questions.description: The decoys are 'thanks', 'hi', and 'sorry'.
questions.explanation: **Farvel** (*fah-VEL*) is the Danish 'farewell' (literally 'fare well', like the English word); the casual hello/bye is `hej` / `hej hej`. Danish is infamous for swallowing its consonants — the spoken language sounds startlingly unlike its tidy spelling, even to other Scandinavians.

questions.title: What's an everyday Icelandic goodbye that English speakers find adorably simple?
questions.description: The decoys are 'thanks', 'hello', and 'excuse me'.
questions.explanation: **Bless** — or the doubled `bless bless` — is the everyday Icelandic goodbye, descended from a blessing ('[God] bless [you]'). Thanks is `takk` (`takk fyrir`, 'thanks for [it]'). Icelandic is so conservative that speakers can still read medieval sagas — but its goodbye is delightfully easy.

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Welsh?
questions.description: The decoys are 'hello', 'bye', and 'good morning'.
questions.explanation: **Diolch** (*DEE-olkh* — that final `ch` is a throaty sound like Scottish 'loch') is thanks; `diolch yn fawr` is 'thank you very much'. The casual goodbye is `hwyl` (which also means 'fun/spirit'), and `bore da` is 'good morning'. Welsh is a living Celtic language, cousin to Irish and Breton.

questions.title: The Hungarian hi/bye **szia** is often linked to the Latin `servus` ('servant') — making it a relative of which Italian word?
questions.explanation: **Szia** (*SEE-yah*) is the casual Hungarian hi *and* bye, commonly traced to Latin `servus` ('[your] servant') — the same humble-flattery origin as Italian `ciao`. (It is a happy coincidence that it sounds like English 'see ya'.) Thanks is `köszönöm`; the formal goodbye `viszontlátásra` is a real jaw-stretcher meaning 'until we see again'.

questions.title: The Romanian **salut** (a casual hi/bye) comes from a Latin word meaning…
questions.explanation: **Salut** (*sah-LOOT*) is from Latin `salus` ('health'), so Romanians, like the French, literally toss you a 'health!' as a casual hi or bye. Romanian is the Romance language stranded among Slavic neighbours, so for thanks you will hear both the native `mulțumesc` and the French-borrowed `mersi`.

questions.title: The Tamil greeting **வணக்கம் (vanakkam)** expresses…
questions.explanation: **வணக்கம்** (*vuh-NUH-kum*) is a respectful all-purpose greeting (hi and bye) rooted in `vaṇaṅku`, 'to bow / pay homage' — close in spirit to `namaste`, palms together. Tamil is one of the world's oldest living languages, with a literary history stretching back over two millennia. Thanks is `நன்றி (nandri)`.

questions.title: The Bengali **ধন্যবাদ (dhonnobad)** is a 'thank you' that, like its Hindi twin, is often felt as…

options.text: Quite formal — casual speakers may use English 'thanks'

questions.explanation: **ধন্যবাদ** (*DHON-no-bad*) — same Sanskrit source as Hindi `dhanyavād` — is correct but can sound formal among friends and family, where unspoken gratitude is the norm; casually many Bengalis just say the English 'thanks'. Bengali is among the world's most-spoken languages, with a Nobel-winning literary tradition (Tagore wrote in it).

questions.title: How do you say **thank you** in Mongolian?
questions.description: The decoys are 'hi', 'goodbye', and 'yes'.
questions.explanation: **Баярлалаа** (*bah-yar-LAH*) is thanks, built on `bayar` ('joy') — so you are basically saying the favour brought you joy. The hello is `сайн байна уу (sain baina uu)`, 'are you well?', and the goodbye `баяртай (bayartai)` shares that same 'joy' root: 'be with joy'.

questions.title: In Esperanto, the constructed international language, **hello** is **saluton**. How is its 'thank you' formed?

options.text: Dankon — a Germanic root plus a regular Esperanto ending

questions.explanation: **Saluton** ('hi') and **dankon** ('thanks') show off Esperanto's design: `dank-` echoes German `danke` / English 'thanks', and the `-on` ending marks it grammatically — regular, predictable, no exceptions. Zamenhof built the language in the 1880s hoping a neutral, easy second tongue might ease international friction. Goodbye is `ĝis (la revido)`, 'until (the re-seeing)'.

questions.title: Select **every** word below that can mean **both** 'hello' AND 'goodbye'.
questions.explanation: `Aloha`, `ciao`, `shalom` and casual Korean `annyeong` are all reversible — context tells you whether someone is coming or going (Thai `sawatdi` and Māori `kia ora` belong here too). The traps are one-way: `arigatō` is only 'thanks', `gracias` only 'thank you'. Languages love a good multipurpose word.

questions.title: Which of these goodbyes literally commend you **'to God'**?
questions.explanation: `Adiós`, `adeus`, `adieu` and `addio` (whence Greek `antío`) all pack `a + Dios/Deus/Dio`, 'to God'. English 'goodbye' secretly joins them — it is a crushed 'God be with ye'. The traps do not: `sayōnara` is 'if it must be so', `ciao` is 'your servant'.

questions.title: Which goodbyes literally mean some version of **'until we see each other again'**?
questions.explanation: These four all promise a re-meeting — and Dutch `tot ziens`, Polish `do widzenia`, Finnish `näkemiin`, Czech `na shledanou` and Chinese `zàijiàn` pile in too. The traps mean other things: `adiós` is 'to God', `aloha` is 'presence of breath'. 'See you again' may be humanity's single most popular way to part.

questions.title: Which greetings are, at root, a wish for **health or wellness**?
questions.explanation: `Salvē`, `zdravstvuyte`, `kia ora` and `salut` all literally wish you health or life. The traps mean other things: `bonjour` is plainly 'good day', and `sawubona` is 'I see you'. Toasting someone's health turns out to be one of our favourite ways to say hi.

questions.title: Which languages use a **different goodbye depending on whether you're the one leaving or the one staying**?
questions.explanation: Korean (`gaseyo` / `gyeseyo`), Turkish (`güle güle` / `hoşça kal`), Zulu (`hamba kahle` / `sala kahle`) and Māori (`haere rā` / `e noho rā`) all pick the farewell by who moves — and Malay/Indonesian (`selamat jalan` / `selamat tinggal`) does too. Spanish and French just say `adiós` / `au revoir` to everyone. Four unrelated languages, one tidy idea.

questions.title: Which greetings or farewells come from a root meaning **'peace'**?
questions.explanation: The Semitic root `š-l-m` gives Hebrew `shalom` and Arabic/Persian `salām` (and, borrowed, Malay `selamat` and Tagalog `salamat`), all 'peace/safety'. Korean `annyeong` reaches the same idea from a different family — Sino-Korean `安寧`, 'tranquillity'. The traps are unrelated: `merci` is 'reward/mercy', `hej` is just 'hey'.

questions.title: Which casual greetings began as a flowery way of saying **'[I am] your servant'**?
questions.explanation: `Ciao` (from Venetian `s-ciào vostro`), `servus` (Latin for 'servant'), and Hungarian `szia` (usually traced to that same `servus`) all started as 'at your service' grovelling that mellowed into a breezy hi/bye. `Hola` and `hej` carry no such baggage — they are just hellos.

questions.title: In which of these languages do many speakers commonly use the **French 'merci'** for everyday thanks?
questions.explanation: French was a prestige language in both Qajar-era Iran and 19th-century Romania, so `merci` slipped into daily use in Persian and Romanian (alongside their native words). Japanese (`arigatō`) and Swahili (`asante`) kept their own. Borrowing a 'thank you' is surprisingly common — gratitude travels light.

questions.title: Which of these greetings literally describe a **physical gesture or perception of the other person** (bowing, seeing)?
questions.explanation: `Namaste` ('I bow to you'), `sawubona` ('I see you') and Tamil `vanakkam` (rooted in 'to bow') each describe acknowledging the other person bodily — unlike time-of-day hellos such as `bonjour`. And `spasibo` is a thank-you ('God save you'), not a greeting at all — a deliberate trap.

questions.title: Some languages turn a greeting into a goodbye just by **doubling or tweaking it**. Which examples are real?
questions.explanation: Finnish doubles `moi` into `moi moi`, Swedish tags `hej` into `hej då`, and Mandarin happily reduplicates the English import as `bái-bái`. French and Arabic do not work this way — repeating `bonjour` or `marhaba` just sounds like you said hello twice. Reduplication is a real and very cute farewell strategy.

questions.title: **Type** the Hawaiian word that means hello, goodbye, *and* love.
questions.explanation: **Aloha** (*ah-LOH-hah*) literally means 'the sharing of breath' (`alo` + `hā`) and carries love and compassion — which is why it works as hello, goodbye and an expression of affection all at once.

questions.title: **Type** the Hawaiian word for 'thank you' (the one on Hawaii's trash cans).
questions.explanation: **Mahalo** (*mah-HAH-loh*) is Hawaiian for thanks; `mahalo nui loa` is 'thank you very much'. Don't confuse it with `aloha` — a classic tourist mix-up.

questions.title: **Type** the Italian word — used for both 'hi' and 'bye' — that began as a phrase meaning '[I am] your servant'.
questions.explanation: **Ciao** came from Venetian `s-ciào vostro` ('I am your slave/servant'). It is now borrowed worldwide and even reshaped into Portuguese `tchau`.

questions.title: **Type** the casual Czech greeting that sounds just like a pirate's 'ahoy'.
questions.explanation: **Ahoj** (*AH-hoy*) is the Czech hi/bye that uncannily matches the nautical English shout — and probably did reach Czech via sailors. The formal version is `dobrý den`.

questions.title: **Type** the Swahili word for 'thank you' (as in Rafiki's 'Asante ___, squash banana').
questions.explanation: **Asante** (*ah-SAHN-teh*) is Swahili thanks; `asante sana` is 'thank you very much'. The Lion King is packed with real Swahili: `simba` (lion), `rafiki` (friend), `hakuna matata` (no worries).

questions.title: **Type** the Māori greeting (now everyday New Zealand English) that literally means 'be well / have life'.
questions.explanation: **Kia ora** (*kee-ah OR-ah*) means 'be well / have life' and does the work of hello, thanks, cheers and 'I agree'. One of the most useful two-word phrases on Earth.

questions.title: **Type** the Hebrew word that means 'peace' and serves as both hello and goodbye.
questions.explanation: **Shalom** (*shah-LOHM*) means 'peace/wholeness' and works coming or going. It shares the Semitic peace-root with Arabic `salām` and Persian `salām`.

questions.title: **Type** the French word for 'thank you' — also borrowed into Persian and Romanian.
questions.explanation: **Merci** (*mehr-SEE*) descends from Latin `merces` ('reward'), and is so handy that Iranians and Romanians adopted it for daily use. Add `beaucoup` for 'thanks a lot'.

questions.title: **Type** the Spanish word for 'thank you'.
questions.explanation: **Gracias** (*GRAH-syahs*) comes from Latin `gratia` ('grace, favour') — kin to Italian `grazie` and English *gratitude*. Always plural in form.

questions.title: **Type** the German word for 'thank you' (one word).
questions.explanation: **Danke** (*DAHN-kuh*) is German thanks; `danke schön` is 'thank you kindly'. Its Slavic cousins are Polish `dziękuję` and Czech `děkuji`. Don't mix it up with `bitte` ('please / you're welcome').

questions.title: **Type** the Finnish word for 'thank you'.
questions.explanation: **Kiitos** (*KEE-tohs*) is Finnish thanks. Finnish is Uralic, not Indo-European, so it shares this word with neither Swedish nor Russian — it stands proudly on its own.

questions.title: English 'goodbye' is a worn-down contraction of 'God be with ___'. **Type** the missing old word.
questions.explanation: 'Goodbye' began as **'God be with ye'** (`ye` being the old plural 'you'), squeezed over centuries into one word and nudged toward 'good-' by 'good day' / 'good night'. So even a brisk English 'bye' hides a blessing, right alongside Spanish `adiós` and Greek `antío`.
